zh.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John於1471年出生在英國貝德福爾布勒左。她於1496年與Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare在愛爾蘭結婚。他們至少是5個兒子的父母。她於1495年住在英國維爾爾萊迪亞德 特雷格茲。她去世於1516年6月28日在愛爾蘭享年45歲。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John于1471年出生在英国貝德福爾布勒左。她于1496年与Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare在爱尔兰结婚。他们至少是5个儿子的父母。她于1495年住在英国維爾爾萊迪亞德 特雷格茲。她去世于1516年6月28日在爱尔兰享年45岁。

nameMeanings.name: St
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: 1  English (of Norman origin): habitational name from any of the numerous places in France so called from the dedication of their churches to St. Jean (  see  John  ).
2  Americanized form of French   St  .   Jean  .

variants.name: John
meanings: English and Welsh: ultimately from the Hebrew personal name Yoḥanan ‘Jehovah has favored (me with a son)’ or ‘may Jehovah favor (this child)’. This personal name was adopted into Latin (via Greek) as Johannes, and has enjoyed enormous popularity in Europe throughout the Christian era, being given in honor of Saint John the Baptist, precursor of Christ, and of Saint John the Evangelist, author of the fourth gospel, as well as the nearly one thousand other Christian saints who bore the name. Some of the principal forms of the personal name in other languages are: Welsh Ieuan, Evan, Siôn, and Ioan; Scottish Ia(i)n; Irish Séan; German Johann, Johannes; Dutch and Slavic Jan; French Jean; Italian Giovanni; Spanish Juan; Portuguese João; Greek Iōannēs (vernacular Giannis, Yannis); Russian Ivan. There were a number of different forms of the name in Middle English, including Jan(e), a male name (see Jayne ); Jen (see Jenkin ); Jon(e) (see Jones ); and Han(n) (see Hann ). By the beginning of the 14th century John rivalled William in popularity and has always been a favorite name. Johan became Jo(h)n, and another Old French form Jehan was shortened to Jan and Jen, giving rise to Old French and Middle English diminutives such as Jonin, Janin, and Jenin. More common in Middle English were Jankin, Jonkin, and Jenkin, which were Middle Dutch pet forms introduced after the Conquest by Flemish and Picard settlers. The most common pet form of John was Jack, another borrowing from Flemish and Picard usage. Han may sometimes have been a short form of Johan but was more usually a pet form of Henry. There were also various Middle English feminine versions of this name (e.g. Joan, Jehan), some of which were indistinguishable from masculine forms. The distinction on grounds of gender between John and Joan was not firmly established in English until the 17th century. It was even later that Jean and Jane were specialized as specifically female names in English; bearers of these surnames and their derivatives are more likely to derive them from a male ancestor than a female. As a surname in the British Isles, John is particularly frequent in Wales, where it is a late formation representing Welsh Siôn rather than the older form Ieuan (which gave rise to the surname Evan ). In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed many cognates from other languages, e.g. Assyrian/Chaldean Youkhana , French Jean , Hungarian János (see Janos ), Slovenian Janež and Janeš (see Janes ), Czech Jan , Albanian Gjoni , and their derivatives (see examples at Johnson ). The name John is also found among Christians in southern India (compare Ninan and Yohannan ), but since South Indians traditionally do not have hereditary surnames, the southern Indian name was in most cases registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US.
German: from a North German and Silesian variant of the personal name   Johannes  . This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine). Compare   Yohn  .

variants.name: Jean
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Francois, Marcel, Yves, Andre, Jacques, Georges, Michel, Philippe, Serge, Yvon, Cecile.

French, Walloon, West Indian (mainly Haiti), Mauritian, and English (of French Huguenot origin): from the male personal name Jean, French form of John . This is one of the most frequent surnames in Haiti (along with Joseph and Pierre ). It is also established elsewhere in the West Indies (e.g. in the Dominican Republic, Saint Lucia, Saint Martin, and Bahamas).
English: habitational name for someone from Genoa in Italy; compare   Janeway  .

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Welsh: ultimately from the Hebrew personal name Yoḥanan ‘Jehovah has favored (me with a son)’ or ‘may Jehovah favor (this child)’. This personal name was adopted into Latin (via Greek) as Johannes, and has enjoyed enormous popularity in Europe throughout the Christian era, being given in honor of Saint John the Baptist, precursor of Christ, and of Saint John the Evangelist, author of the fourth gospel, as well as the nearly one thousand other Christian saints who bore the name. The most common pet form of John was Jack, another borrowing from Flemish and Picard usage. Han may sometimes have been a short form of Johan but was more usually a pet form of Henry. There were also various Middle English feminine versions of this name (e.g. Joan, Jehan), some of which were indistinguishable from masculine forms. The distinction on grounds of gender between John and Joan was not firmly established in English until the 17th century. It was even later that Jean and Jane were specialized as specifically female names in English; bearers of these surnames and their derivatives are more likely to derive them from a male ancestor than a female. As a surname in the British Isles, John is particularly frequent in Wales, where it is a late formation representing Welsh Siôn rather than the older form Ieuan (which gave rise to the surname Evan ). In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed many cognates from other languages, e.g. Assyrian/Chaldean Youkhana , French Jean , Hungarian János (see Janos ), Slovenian Janež and Janeš (see Janes ), Czech Jan , Albanian Gjoni , and their derivatives (see examples at Johnson ). The name John is also found among Christians in southern India (compare Ninan and Yohannan ), but since South Indians traditionally do not have hereditary surnames, the southern Indian name was in most cases registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US.
German: from a North German and Silesian variant of the personal name   Johannes  . This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine). Compare   Yohn  .

variants.name: Ancar
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Francois, Alphonse, Etienne, Fabien, Mireille.

Probably an altered form of French (Pas-de-Calais, Nord) Ancart, itself a variant of Hancart and Hanecart: from a pet form (double diminutive) of the personal name Han, a short form of French Jehan and Flemish Johan(nes) (see John ).

variants.name: Bongiovanni
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Mario, Antonio, Dante, Salvatore, Santo, Antonino, Domenic, Gaetano, Geno, Giacomo, Gilda, Luciano, Nino.
Italian: from a personal name composed of the elements bon ‘good’ + Giovanni, an equivalent of John .

variants.name: Di Giovanna
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Gaspar, Alfonso, Carlo, Domenic, Gaetano, Liborio, Nunzio, Pietro, Rosario, Sal, Salvatore.
Italian: metronymic from the female personal name Giovanna, an equivalent of Joan (see John ).

variants.name: Anes
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Angel, Dionisio, Esperanza, Gilberto, Ines, Juanita, Luis, Luisa, Manuel, Manuela, Pedro.  Portuguese Joao.
Portuguese and Galician: patronymic from a shortened form of the Latin personal name Johannes (Portuguese João, Galician Xoán; see John ).

variants.name: Burhans
meanings: German: from Middle Low German būr ‘farmer, peasant’ + the personal name Hans, a short form of Johannes (see John ), hence a distinguishing occupational name ‘farmer John’ with Hans being a very frequent name. See also Burian .

variants.name: Breihan
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Kurt.
German: distinguishing nickname for a (beer) brewer, from Middle High German briuw(er) ‘brew(er)’ + a short form of the personal name Johann(es) (see John ).

variants.name: Jayne
meanings: English (Gloucestershire) and Welsh (Monmouthshire): from the Middle English male personal name Jan(e), from Old French Jehan, a variant of Johan, which gave rise to the common alternative John . Sometimes the name may also arise from the Welsh variant Ieun of Welsh Ieuan, likewise equivalent to John (compare Evan ).

variants.name: Di Giovanni
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Salvatore, Vito, Rocco, Sal, Aldo, Carlo, Gino, Giuseppe, Guido, Agostino, Carmela.
Italian: patronymic from the personal name Giovanni, an equivalent of John . Compare De Giovanni , Dejohn , and Giovanni .

variants.name: Eanes
meanings: English (Hampshire): probably a variant of Heanes with loss of initial H-, itself a variant of Heane, with post-medieval excrescent -s, a nickname from Middle English hen(e), heine ‘poor, needy, wretched’ (Old English hēan). Eanes is now rare in Britain, and in the US is found predominantly in VA.
Portuguese and Galician: patronymic from the Latin personal name Johannes (Portuguese João, Galician Xoán; see John ).

variants.name: Ene
meanings: Romanian: from a derivative of the personal name Ioan (see John ).
West African (Nigeria): unexplained.

nameMeanings.name: Elizabeth
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: The usual spelling of   Elisabeth   in English. It is recorded in the medieval period, but was made popular by being borne by  Queen Elizabeth I  of England (  1533–1603 ). In the 20th century it again became extremely fashionable, partly because it was the name of  Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon  (  1900–2002 ), who in  1936  became Queen Elizabeth as the wife of King George VI, and after his death in  1952  achieved great public affection as Queen Mother for nearly half a century. Even more influentially, it is the name of her daughter  Queen Elizabeth II  (  b. 1926 ).

variants.name: Elisabeth
meanings: The spelling of Elizabeth used in the Authorized Version of the New Testament, and in most modern European languages. This was the name of the mother of John the Baptist (Luke 1:60). Etymologically, the name means ‘God is my oath’, and is therefore identical with Elisheba, the name of the wife of Aaron according to the genealogy at Exodus 6:23. The final element seems to have been altered by association with Hebrew shabbāth ‘Sabbath’.

en.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John was born in 1471, in Bletsoe, Bedfordshire, England. She married Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare in 1496, in Ireland. They were the parents of at least 5 sons. She lived in Lydiard Tregoze, Wiltshire, England in 1495. She died on 28 June 1516, in Ireland, at the age of 45.

fr.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John est née en 1471, à Bletsoe, Bedfordshire, Angleterre. Elle a épousé Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare en 1496, à Irlande. Ils ont eu au moins 5 fils. Elle a vécu à Lydiard Tregoze, Wiltshire, Angleterre en 1495. Elle est décédée le 28 juin 1516, à Irlande, à l’âge de 45 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John wurde 1471 in Bletsoe, Bedfordshire, England geboren. Sie hat 1496 in Irland Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 5 Söhne. 1495 hat sie in Lydiard Tregoze, Wiltshire, England gelebt. Sie starb am 28. Juni 1516 im Alter von 45 Jahren in Irland.

it.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John è nata il nel 1471 nel Bletsoe, Bedfordshire, England. Lei sposato Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare nel 1496, nel Irlanda. Erano genitori di almeno 5 figli. Vissuto a Lydiard Tregoze, Wiltshire, England nel 1495. Morì il 28 giugno 1516, nel Irlanda, all'età di 45 anni.

ja.Maynooth, Kildare, Ireland: アイルランドコウントイ キルダアメイノオス
ja.Lydiard Tregoze, Wiltshire, England: イギリスウィルトシュアイアリディアルド トレゴズ
ja.Bletsoe, Bedfordshire, England: イギリスベドフォードシュアイアブレトソ
ja.Maynooth Castle, Kildare, Ireland: アイルランドコウントイ キルダアメイノオス
ja.Leap Castle, Offaly, Ireland: アイルランドコウントイ オファリーレアップ
ja.Castle Kildare, Kildare, Ireland: アイルランドコウントイ キルダアキルダア
ja.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St Johnは1471年にイギリスベドフォードシュアイアブレトソで生まれた。1496年にアイルランドでGerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildareと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも5人の息子の親であった。彼女は1495年にはイギリスウィルトシュアイアリディアルド トレゴズに住んでいた。彼女は1516年6月28日にアイルランドで45歳で死去した。

ko.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John(은)는 1471년 영국 베드퍼드실르 블레츠에서 태어났다. 그녀는 1496년 아일랜드에서 Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 다섯 명의 아들을 두었다. 1495년도에 영국 윌츠히르 리디아르드 트레고즈에서 살았다. 그녀는 1516년 6월 28일 아일랜드에서 45세의 나이로 사망했다.

pt.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John nasceu em 1471, em Bletsoe, Bedfordshire, Inglaterra. Ela casou-se com Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare em 1496, em Irlanda. Eles tiveram pelo menos 5 filhos. Ela viveu em Lydiard Tregoze, Wiltshire, Inglaterra em 1495. Ela faleceu em 28 de junho de 1516, em Irlanda, com 45 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John родилась в 1471 г. в местности блецо, бедфордшир, Англия. Она вышла замуж за человека по имени Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare в 1496 г. в местности Ирландия. У них было по крайней мере 5 сыновей. Она жила в местности лидярд трегоз, уилцхир, Англия в 1495 году. Она умерла 28 июня 1516 г. в местности Ирландия в возрасте 45 лет.

es.lifeSummary: Elizabeth St John nació en 1471, en Bletsoe, Bedfordshire, Inglaterra. Se casó con Gerald FitzMaurice FitzGerald, 8th Earl of Kildare en 1496, en Irlanda. Fueron padres de por lo menos 5 hijos. Vivió en Lydiard Tregoze, Wiltshire, Inglaterra en 1495. Murió el 28 de junio de 1516, en Irlanda, a la edad de 45 años.
